planting.note: Direct sow after last frost in spring into dry, sandy or gravelly, well-drained soil in full sun; can also be fall-sown where winters are mild.

directions: Greenthread is a delicate, thread-leaved native of dry prairies and rocky open ground across the Great Plains and Southwest, producing bright yellow, 8-rayed daisy flowers on wiry, much-branched stems from spring through fall. Direct sow seeds in spring after last frost in lean, well-drained, sandy or gravelly soil in full sun — do not enrich the soil, as fertility promotes floppy growth and reduces flowering. The dried stems have long been brewed as a mild, pleasant herbal tea by Pueblo and Navajo peoples, earning it the common name Navajo tea or greenthread tea. Plants self-sow prolifically once established and naturalize readily in open, disturbed, or sandy ground.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall to allow winter cold stratification; seeds can also be sown in spring after a period of cold-moist stratification.

directions: Slender Greenthread is a compact, clump-forming native perennial distinguished from its relative Thelesperma filifolium by its simple, undivided rather than finely dissected leaves; it bears clear golden-yellow daisy flowers singly on slender, upright stems from late spring through summer. Native to dry, rocky, calcareous or sandy open ground and prairies of Texas and adjacent south-central states, it is exceptionally drought-tolerant once established. Direct sow in fall in dry, lean, well-drained soil in full sun, or cold-stratify seeds and sow in spring. Like other greenthreads, the stems and leaves can be dried and brewed as an herbal tea, and established plants are long-lived with minimal care in appropriate well-drained soils.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in sandy, well-drained soil; plants form a rosette in the first year and bolt to flower in the second year, then self-sow to maintain the colony.

directions: Diamond-petal Evening Primrose is a tall, statuesque biennial native to sandy dunes, open disturbed ground, and dry plains of the Great Plains, forming a low, flat gray-green rosette in its first year and shooting up a leafy spike to 6 feet in its second year. The distinctive, large, clear yellow flowers have noticeably diamond- to rhombus-shaped petals 2-3 inches across and open in the evening, attracting hawk moths for pollination before closing by mid-morning. Direct sow in fall in sandy, lean, extremely well-drained soil in full sun, spacing plants generously to allow their architectural second-year form to develop. Plants self-sow reliably in open sandy ground, keeping a self-sustaining colony going year after year without replanting.

planting.note: Direct sow at or just after last frost in spring into dry, lean, well-drained soil in full sun; plants establish quickly in heat and self-sow in open ground.

directions: Lizard-tail Gaura, now reclassified into Oenothera, is a native annual or biennial of dry, open, sandy or rocky ground across Texas and adjacent south-central states, producing long, wand-like spikes of small, 4-petaled white to pale pink flowers that open in the evening and fade to pink or red by the following morning. More compact and freely self-seeding than the widely cultivated Oenothera lindheimeri, it is extremely drought- and heat-tolerant and thrives with complete neglect in hot, dry conditions. Direct sow after last frost in spring in lean, well-drained soil in full sun; plants resent clay or wet soils and will decline quickly if overwatered or crowded. In suitable open, disturbed, or sandy sites, plants self-sow freely and maintain a persistent colony.

planting.note: Transplant in spring after seeds have been cold-stratified for 4-6 weeks; plant into dry, acidic, sandy soil in full sun — avoid clay or amended garden soils.

directions: Florida Phlox is a Florida-endemic native perennial of dry, sandy, acidic pine flatwoods, sandhills, and scrub habitats, bearing clusters of lavender-pink to pale lavender 5-petaled flowers in spring atop narrow, stiff, slightly glandular-hairy stems. It is highly adapted to the extremely infertile, droughty, and acidic sandy soils characteristic of longleaf pine ecosystems where few other flowering perennials can survive, making it an exceptional choice for Florida native gardens and longleaf pine restoration plantings. Cold-stratify seeds for 4-6 weeks before sowing, then start indoors and transplant into dry, sandy, acidic, well-drained soil in full sun in spring. Avoid amended or enriched soils, clay, and any consistent moisture — this species demands conditions that mimic its native scrub habitat to thrive long-term.
sources: Institute for Regional Conservation — Natives For Your Neighborhood
